skill.name: azure-postgres-ts
skill.description: Guides Node.js and TypeScript developers through connecting to Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with node-postgres, connection pools, transactions, typed queries, SSL, and Microsoft Entra ID authentication.
skill.summary: Build TypeScript PostgreSQL clients for Azure with pg, pooling, transactions, and passwordless authentication.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but review shows these are Markdown examples and documentation tables, not executable skill logic. No prompt injection, obfuscation, hidden-file access, credential exfiltration, or malicious network behavior was found; the main residual risk is that users may copy credential-handling database examples into production.

low_findings.title: Credential Handling Appears in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The skill shows process.env values for Azure PostgreSQL host, user, password, port, and Entra ID client ID. This is expected for database connection guidance, but users must avoid committing secrets or logging credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly read database configuration from environment variables in example TypeScript. The context is legitimate connection setup, so the security concern is limited to safe secret handling by downstream users.

low_findings.title: Legitimate External Network Targets in Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes Azure token scope URLs and documentation links. These are appropriate for Microsoft Entra ID authentication and references, with no evidence that the skill itself sends secrets to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network strings are Azure PostgreSQL token scopes and public reference links. No suspicious endpoint, data exfiltration path, or executable downloader was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick execution findings correspond to Markdown inline code and fenced TypeScript or bash examples. They are not Ruby code and do not execute as part of the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible Markdown documentation, including code fences and table cells. There are no separate scripts or runtime hooks in the reported file.

low_findings.title: Other Static Signatures Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The hidden-file, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts map to a token scope, Markdown text, table entries, SQL examples, and pool metrics. No evidence found for hidden-file reads, cryptographic use, or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown, SQL examples, URLs, and monitoring guidance. I did not find executable code that reads hidden files, performs cryptography, or probes the system.

dangerous_patterns.title: Password-Based Database Configuration Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The password-authentication examples read AZURE_POSTGRESQL_PASSWORD from the environment. This is common, but marketplace users should prefer managed identity when available and protect environment variables.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly include a database password field, but they do not expose a hardcoded real secret. The same file also recommends Microsoft Entra ID authentication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Connection String Template Contains Placeholder Password
dangerous_patterns.description: The connection string section includes placeholder credentials to demonstrate PostgreSQL URL format. It is not a real secret, but users should avoid putting real passwords in source-controlled connection strings.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line is clearly a template and sample connection string. The risk is educational misuse rather than malicious content in the skill.

content.value_statement: Connecting TypeScript services to Azure PostgreSQL requires secure configuration, pooling, and transaction patterns. This skill provides practical node-postgres examples for password and Microsoft Entra ID authentication.
actual_capabilities: Shows pg Client and Pool setup for Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
Demonstrates password and Microsoft Entra ID authentication flows.
Provides parameterized query, transaction, and transaction-helper examples.
Explains typed query results with TypeScript generics and pg types.
Documents pool sizing, SSL requirements, token refresh, and common PostgreSQL errors.
limitations: Does not provision Azure PostgreSQL servers or Azure identities.
Does not validate live credentials, firewall rules, or network connectivity.
Examples require adaptation for each application schema and deployment model.
Does not replace a security review for production secret management.

use_cases.title: Add Azure PostgreSQL to a Node.js API
use_cases.description: Create a secure pg Pool configuration with SSL, environment variables, and parameterized queries.

use_cases.title: Adopt Passwordless Database Access
use_cases.description: Use Microsoft Entra ID tokens with Azure PostgreSQL and plan token refresh for long-running services.

use_cases.title: Improve Database Reliability Patterns
use_cases.description: Standardize transactions, pool sizing, error handling, and graceful shutdown in TypeScript services.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to connect a TypeScript Node.js app to Azure PostgreSQL using pg and environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Use a Production Pool
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a production-ready pg Pool configuration for Azure PostgreSQL with SSL, timeouts, and graceful shutdown.

prompt_templates.title: Add Safe Queries and Transactions
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write parameterized pg queries and a transaction helper for an Azure PostgreSQL TypeScript service.

prompt_templates.title: Design Passwordless Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Microsoft Entra ID authentication flow for node-postgres, including token refresh and pool lifecycle handling.

output_examples.input: I need a TypeScript API to connect to Azure PostgreSQL safely.
output_examples.output: A concise connection plan covering required environment variables, SSL configuration, pg Pool settings, and safe parameterized query patterns.

output_examples.input: My service uses managed identity and database tokens expire.
output_examples.output: A passwordless authentication design that obtains Entra ID tokens, refreshes them before expiry, and recreates the pool when needed.

output_examples.input: I want consistent database writes across two tables.
output_examples.output: A transaction workflow that begins a transaction, performs both writes, commits on success, rolls back on failure, and releases the client.

best_practices: Prefer Microsoft Entra ID authentication when your Azure deployment supports it.
Use parameterized queries for all user-provided values.
Close clients and pools with try-finally blocks or shutdown hooks.
anti_patterns: Do not concatenate user input into SQL strings.
Do not commit real database passwords or connection strings.
Do not create a new PostgreSQL client for every request in production.

faq.question: Does this skill create an Azure PostgreSQL server?
faq.answer: No. It explains client-side TypeScript connection patterns for an existing Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server.

faq.question: Which npm package does it use?
faq.answer: It uses pg, also known as node-postgres, with optional @azure/identity support for passwordless authentication.

faq.question: Can it help with Microsoft Entra ID authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to request an Azure PostgreSQL access token and use it as the pg password.

faq.question: Does it cover SQL injection prevention?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends parameterized queries and shows positional parameters for common query patterns.

faq.question: Is this safe for production use?
faq.answer: The examples are production-oriented, but teams must still review secrets, network rules, pool limits, and error logging.

faq.question: Can it type query results in TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples that use pg QueryResult and TypeScript generics for typed rows.
